title: Apples and oranges

content.problemStatement:
Rushitote went to a programming contest to distribute apples and oranges to the contestants.

He has NNN apples and MMM oranges, which need to be divided **equally** amongst the contestants. Find the maximum possible number of contestants such that:


Every contestant gets an equal number of apples; and
Every contestant gets an equal number of oranges.
Note that every fruit with Rushitote must be distributed, there cannot be any left over.

For example, 222 apples and 444 oranges can be distributed equally to two contestants, where each one receives 111 apple and 222 oranges.

However, 222 apples and 555 oranges can only be distributed equally to one contestant.


Input Format

The first line of input will contain a single integer TTT, denoting the number of test cases.
The first and only line of each test case contains two space-separated integers NNN and MMM — the number of apples and oranges, respectively.


Output Format
For each test case, output on a new line the answer: the maximum number of contestants such that everyone receives an equal number of apples and an equal number of oranges.
content.inputFormat: First line contains an integer T, the number of test cases. Each of the next T lines contains two space‑separated integers N and M – the number of apples and oranges respectively.
content.outputFormat: For each test case output a single integer – the maximum possible number of contestants such that every contestant receives the same number of apples and the same number of oranges and no fruit is left undistributed.

sampleTestCases.explanation:
Test case 1: gcd(1,5)=1, only one contestant.
Test case 2: gcd(2,4)=2, two contestants each get 1 apple and 2 oranges.
Test case 3: gcd(4,6)=2, two contestants each get 2 apples and 3 oranges.
gcd(10,20)=10, so ten contestants can each receive 1 apple and 2 oranges.
gcd(100,200)=100, allowing one hundred contestants each with 1 apple and 2 oranges.

solution.approach: The number of contestants must divide both N and M exactly, otherwise some fruit would remain. Hence the answer is the greatest common divisor of N and M. Computing gcd with the Euclidean algorithm runs in O(log min(N,M)) time per test case, which easily meets the limits.

code.Java: import java.io.*;import java.util.*;public class Main{private static long gcd(long a,long b){while(b!=0){long r=a%b;a=b;b=r;}return a;}public static void main(String[] args)throws Exception{BufferedReader br=new BufferedReader(new InputStreamReader(System.in));StringBuilder sb=new StringBuilder();int T=Integer.parseInt(br.readLine().trim());for(int i=0;i<T;i++){StringTokenizer st=new StringTokenizer(br.readLine());long N=Long.parseLong(st.nextToken());long M=Long.parseLong(st.nextToken());sb.append(gcd(N,M)).append('\n');}System.out.print(sb.toString());}}
